introSection: MonteZOOMa: The Forbidden Fortress, formerly known as Montezooma's Revenge, is a steel launched shuttle roller coaster located at Knott's Berry Farm in Buena Park, California, USA. The ride was built by Schwarzkopf and is the last operating Schwarzkopf Shuttle Loop in the United States. It is also the oldest operating roller coaster at Knott's Berry Farm.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Montezooma's Revenge opened on May 26, 1978.

Contrary to popular belief, the coaster is not named after the disease sometimes called Montezuma's Revenge, but rather a band under the same name that performed at the park.

The ride had its two millionth rider in 1979. The rider was awarded with two million Mexican centavos (approximately $800 USD) and was celebrated with a live mariachi band.

The ride was repainted for the 2017 season.

The ride was designated an ACE Roller Coaster Landmark by American Coaster Enthusiasts in 2019.

On January 7, 2022, it was announced that the ride would close on January 10 for a major refurbishment. However, the next day, the park stated that it would stay open for some time longer due to popular demand.

The flywheel launch system and some sections of track have now been removed. The original train was donated to the National Roller Coaster Museum in Plainview, Texas, USA. The park later donated the last car with the clutch plate for the flywheel, part of the loop track, and the entry sign from the front of the queue line to the museum.

The work was originally expected to be completed by 2023. However, the park has since announced the ride would not reopen in 2023 with no expected opening date given. On March 1, 2024, it was announced that the ride would reopen in 2025.

New track pieces started to be installed in June 2025.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: Montezooma's Revenge has a single vertical loop which the train passes through twice: once forwards and once backwards. The nearby roller coaster Jaguar! passes through Montezooma's Revenge's vertical loop.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: On the evening of August 31, 2001, a woman was taken to hospital after reportedly suffering a seizure while riding Montezooma's Revenge. She died the following morning. An investigation found the roller coaster to be mechanically safe, and the cause of death was a brain aneurysm aggravated by a pre-existing condition. Montezooma's Revenge reopened on September 6.

fullText:
MonteZOOMa: The Forbidden Fortress, formerly known as Montezooma's Revenge, is a steel launched shuttle roller coaster located at Knott's Berry Farm in Buena Park, California, USA. The ride was built by Schwarzkopf and is the last operating Schwarzkopf Shuttle Loop in the United States. It is also the oldest operating roller coaster at Knott's Berry Farm.

Montezooma's Revenge opened on May 26, 1978.

Contrary to popular belief, the coaster is not named after the disease sometimes called Montezuma's Revenge, but rather a band under the same name that performed at the park.

The ride had its two millionth rider in 1979. The rider was awarded with two million Mexican centavos (approximately $800 USD) and was celebrated with a live mariachi band.

The ride was repainted for the 2017 season.

The ride was designated an ACE Roller Coaster Landmark by American Coaster Enthusiasts in 2019.

On January 7, 2022, it was announced that the ride would close on January 10 for a major refurbishment. However, the next day, the park stated that it would stay open for some time longer due to popular demand.

The flywheel launch system and some sections of track have now been removed. The original train was donated to the National Roller Coaster Museum in Plainview, Texas, USA. The park later donated the last car with the clutch plate for the flywheel, part of the loop track, and the entry sign from the front of the queue line to the museum.

The work was originally expected to be completed by 2023. However, the park has since announced the ride would not reopen in 2023 with no expected opening date given. On March 1, 2024, it was announced that the ride would reopen in 2025.

New track pieces started to be installed in June 2025.

Montezooma's Revenge has a single vertical loop which the train passes through twice: once forwards and once backwards. The nearby roller coaster Jaguar! passes through Montezooma's Revenge's vertical loop.



On the evening of August 31, 2001, a woman was taken to hospital after reportedly suffering a seizure while riding Montezooma's Revenge. She died the following morning. An investigation found the roller coaster to be mechanically safe, and the cause of death was a brain aneurysm aggravated by a pre-existing condition. Montezooma's Revenge reopened on September 6.
